threats.title: Complete Pool Drain via burn() Reentrancy
threats.hypothesis_description: The burn() function executes external token transfers before updating the user's liquidity balance, total supply, and pool reserves. This allows an attacker using a token with callback capabilities (e.g., ERC777) to reenter the burn() function and repeatedly extract tokens using the same liquidity balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: Cross-function and same-function reentrancy in the withdrawal phase is a critical operational threat that can instantly drain all pool reserves without requiring special privileges.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; vulnerability exists in core logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External calls (IERC20.transfer) executed before state updates (liquidityBalances, totalSupply, pool.reserves)

preconditions: Pool contains at least one token with callback capabilities (e.g., ERC777, ERC1363) or a malicious token contract
Attacker has minted some LP tokens

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State updates for balances and reserves occur after external token transfers in burn()
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after liquidity is deposited

operational_questions: Can the attacker reenter burn() multiple times in a single transaction?
Can the attacker reenter executeFeeSwap() during the burn() callback to exploit stale reserves?
validation_requirements: Deploy test pool with ERC777 token
Attempt reentrancy on burn() to verify multiple withdrawals from single deposit

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create pool with a callback-enabled token and deposit liquidity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: In the token fallback function, call burn() again before the first call updates state
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat until pool is drained of both tokens

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete loss of all liquidity provider funds in the affected pool

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Smart contract development for reentrancy exploit

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple burn events in the same transaction from the same address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Pool reserves dropping to zero while total supply remains non-zero
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Use of custom or ERC777 tokens in new pools

threats.title: Validator Token Drain via executeFeeSwap() Reentrancy
threats.hypothesis_description: executeFeeSwap() calls transferFrom on the userToken before decrementing reserveValidatorToken. A malicious userToken can trigger a callback to reenter executeFeeSwap(), bypassing the reserve sufficiency check repeatedly to drain validatorTokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: State updates must precede external calls to prevent attackers from exploiting stale state checks during reentrant execution.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; vulnerability exists in core logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External call (IERC20.transferFrom) executed before state update (pool.reserveValidatorToken -= amountOut)

preconditions: userToken is a malicious contract or has callback capabilities

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: reserveValidatorToken is decremented after the external transferFrom call

operational_questions: Does the reserve sufficiency check pass during reentrancy?
How many reentrant calls are required to drain the pool?
validation_requirements: Simulate executeFeeSwap with a reentrant userToken
Verify validatorToken balance is drained while internal reserves remain artificially high until the final return

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy malicious userToken contract with reentrant transferFrom hook
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Create pool and wait for legitimate LPs to deposit validatorTokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: In the transferFrom hook, reenter executeFeeSwap() repeatedly to extract validatorTokens

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple executeFeeSwap calls in a single transaction
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: validatorToken balance depleted while internal reserves show otherwise
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Creation of pools with unverified userTokens

threats.title: Phantom Reserve Inflation via rebalanceSwap() Reentrancy
threats.hypothesis_description: rebalanceSwap() optimistically updates pool reserves before calling transferFrom on the validatorToken. A malicious validatorToken can trigger a callback during transferFrom, allowing the attacker to reenter burn() and extract tokens based on artificially inflated reserves.
threats.investigation_rationale: Optimistic state updates without reentrancy guards allow attackers to leverage phantom balances in other protocol functions.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; vulnerability exists in core logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State updates (pool.reserveValidatorToken += amountIn) executed before external call (IERC20.transferFrom)

preconditions: Attacker holds LP tokens for the pool

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Reserves are incremented before the tokens are actually transferred into the contract

operational_questions: Can burn() be successfully executed during the rebalanceSwap transferFrom callback?
Does the inflated reserveValidatorToken result in excess withdrawal amounts?
validation_requirements: Call burn() during the callback and verify excess extraction

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain LP tokens for a pool with a reentrant validatorToken
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call rebalanceSwap() with a large amountIn
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: During the transferFrom callback, call burn() to withdraw liquidity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The withdrawal calculation uses the inflated reserves, granting the attacker more tokens than they are entitled to

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: rebalanceSwap and burn called in the same transaction
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large rebalanceSwap followed immediately by burn

threats.title: LP Share Dilution via mint() Reentrancy
threats.hypothesis_description: mint() calls transferFrom on the validatorToken before updating reserveValidatorToken and totalSupply. A callback during transferFrom allows reentering mint() with stale, smaller pool reserves, resulting in the attacker receiving disproportionately more LP shares.
threats.investigation_rationale: Incorrect ordering of state updates and external calls in deposit functions allows manipulation of share pricing.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; vulnerability exists in core logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External call (IERC20.transferFrom) executed before state updates (pool.reserveValidatorToken, totalSupply)

preconditions: Pool has already been initialized (first deposit completed)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: transferFrom occurs before LP shares are calculated and state is updated

operational_questions: How much excess LP can be minted via this method?
Does this allow the attacker to steal value from existing LPs?
validation_requirements: Compare LP shares received vs expected shares without reentrancy

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Call mint() with a reentrant validatorToken
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: During the transferFrom callback, call mint() again
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The second mint uses the pre-first-mint reserves for calculation, yielding more shares
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Burn the inflated LP shares to extract value from other LPs

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple mint calls in the same transaction
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Flash loan followed by multiple mints and a burn

threats.title: Guaranteed Pool Depletion via Fixed-Rate Arbitrage
threats.hypothesis_description: The AMM uses hardcoded fixed exchange rates (0.997 and 1.0015) without any price oracle or bonding curve. If the external market price of the paired stablecoins diverges from this fixed rate (e.g., a depeg event), arbitrageurs can atomically drain the more valuable token from the pool at the expense of LPs.
threats.investigation_rationale: Fixed-rate AMMs without dynamic pricing or slippage curves are fundamentally vulnerable to market price divergences.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; vulnerability exists in core logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Fixed rate calculations (amountOut = amountIn * M / SCALE) without price oracle or slippage bounds

preconditions: Market price of userToken vs validatorToken diverges from 1:1 by more than the 0.3% fee

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Pricing is entirely deterministic and ignores external market conditions
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any stablecoin depeg or market volatility event

operational_questions: What is the exact threshold for profitable arbitrage?
Can LPs withdraw funds before arbitrageurs drain the pool during a depeg?
validation_requirements: Model pool behavior during a 1% depeg event

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor external markets for price divergence between pool tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: When divergence exceeds fee threshold, execute massive swap to extract the more valuable token
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Sell extracted token on external market for profit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat until pool is drained of the valuable token

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete depletion of one side of the pool

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Pool heavily imbalanced toward one token
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Arbitrage bots targeting the pool during volatility

threats.title: Silent Transfer Failures Corrupting Pool State
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract uses standard IERC20 transfer/transferFrom calls without verifying the boolean return value. If a pool is created with a token that returns false on failure instead of reverting (e.g., ZRX, USDT on some chains), transfers can fail silently while the contract still updates its internal reserves, allowing attackers to steal tokens or permanently corrupt accounting.
threats.investigation_rationale: Failure to handle non-standard ERC20 return values leads to severe accounting desynchronization and potential theft.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; vulnerability exists in core logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Direct use of IERC20.transfer/transferFrom without checking boolean return value

preconditions: Pool uses a token that returns false on failure instead of reverting

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No SafeERC20 wrapper used, and return values of transfer calls are ignored

operational_questions: Which specific stablecoins intended for this AMM exhibit this behavior?
Can an attacker force a false return (e.g., via zero allowance) to exploit this?
validation_requirements: Deploy pool with a non-reverting token
Attempt swap with zero allowance and verify state updates despite transfer failure

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call rebalanceSwap() with zero allowance for validatorToken
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: transferFrom fails silently, but reserveValidatorToken is incremented and userToken is transferred out

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Theft of pool funds and accounting corruption

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Swaps executing without corresponding token transfer events
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Reserves heavily desynchronized from actual contract balances
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated swaps from addresses with zero token balances

threats.title: Pool Insolvency via Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract updates internal reserves using the nominal transfer amounts (amountIn) rather than measuring the actual balance changes. If a fee-on-transfer token is used, the contract will receive fewer tokens than recorded in the reserves. This leads to a progressive deficit, eventually causing legitimate user withdrawals and swaps to revert due to insufficient actual balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: Accounting mechanisms that do not measure actual balance changes are vulnerable to insolvency when interacting with fee-on-transfer tokens.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; vulnerability exists in core logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State reserves updated using nominal amountIn rather than measuring actual balance changes

preconditions: Pool is created using a fee-on-transfer token

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Reserves are incremented by exact function parameters rather than balance deltas
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Gradual over time as pool is used

operational_questions: Are fee-on-transfer tokens expected to be used in this protocol?
How quickly does the pool become insolvent based on typical swap volumes?
validation_requirements: Deploy pool with a 1% fee-on-transfer token
Execute several swaps and verify that reserves exceed actual balances
Attempt to burn all liquidity and observe revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users interact with pool, causing reserves to artificially inflate compared to actual balances
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Early LPs withdraw, taking a disproportionate share of the actual balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Late LPs attempt to withdraw but transaction reverts due to insufficient contract balance

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent locking of late LPs' funds
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires external funding to cover deficit

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed burn or swap transactions due to insufficient balance
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Early LPs rushing to exit pools with fee-on-transfer tokens

threats.title: Accounting Desynchronization via Rebasing Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The AMM relies entirely on internal reserve accounting rather than checking actual token balances. If a rebasing token is used, positive rebases will permanently lock the excess tokens in the contract, while negative rebases will cause the actual balance to drop below the recorded reserves, bricking pool operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Strict internal accounting without balance reconciliation is incompatible with elastic supply tokens.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; vulnerability exists in core logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Internal reserve accounting (reserveUserToken, reserveValidatorToken) used instead of actual token balances

preconditions: Pool is created using an elastic supply (rebasing) token

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No mechanism exists to sync internal reserves with actual contract balances

operational_questions: Are rebasing tokens supported or expected?
Can an attacker force a rebase to intentionally brick a pool?
validation_requirements: Deploy pool with a rebasing token
Trigger a negative rebase and verify that burn() reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for or trigger a negative rebase event
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Actual contract balance drops below internal reserves
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Subsequent swaps or burns revert when attempting to transfer out tokens the contract no longer holds

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Denial of service for pool withdrawals and swaps

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Mismatch between reserves and actual balances
